topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an aggressive, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I kept running a hand over my meticulously combed hair, the strands feeling suddenly brittle against my palm. Every time Mark mentioned the Senior Manager role, my stomach performed a frantic, uneven flip. I watched him across the cubicle partition, his confident posture radiating an aura of assured success, and I felt a cold sweat prickle the back of my neck. *This is it,* a small, panicked voice whispered in my head. I started organizing my desk supplies with an almost violent precision, stacking the paper clips until they formed a perfect, unbreachable tower. If I moved too quickly, if I made a wrong move, the entire delicate structure of my composure might collapse. I took a deep, shallow breath, the air catching painfully in my chest, and forced a smile that felt like cracking plaster.

[story 2]

I kept my gaze fixed on the spreadsheet on my monitor, refusing to look up when Sarah approached my desk. The sound of her footsteps, light and deliberate, echoed in the otherwise quiet corner of the department. My hands, resting on the keyboard, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. It wasn't just nerves; it was a physical seizing up, a strange tightness in my throat that made swallowing feel like swallowing sandpaper. When she finally spoke, her voice was warm and encouraging, detailing the scope of the new position. I nodded, my movements stiff, trying desperately to appear engaged and competent. Internally, a siren was blaring—a frantic calculation of every possible failure point. If I stumbled over a word, if my prepared talking points dissolved into static, the silence that followed would feel deafening, absolute. I focused intently on a stray smudge on the monitor screen, willing myself to remain utterly still.

[story 3]

The air in the conference room felt thick, heavy, suffocating. I adjusted my tie for the tenth time, the silk feeling suddenly abrasive against my collar. When the announcement about the promotion candidates was finally made, the sound seemed distant, muffled, as if I were submerged underwater. My vision narrowed to a single point on the polished mahogany table. My palms were slick; I could feel the moisture gathering beneath my skin, making it hard to grip the water glass. I watched Mark exchange a knowing glance with the executive, and a wave of intense pressure washed over me, squeezing my lungs. I wanted to speak, to interject a clarifying point, but my mouth was dry, coated with a sudden, unwelcome film. I just sat, perfectly still, my jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars, waiting for the verdict to drop.
